title: Post-call: MediaStream pricing/target $/1M tokens and competitive extract
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Alex Johnson <alex.j@mediastream.io>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-06-18T14:02:00-07:00
Subject: Post-call recap: MediaStream pricing / target $/1M tokens

Alex — thanks for taking the time today. Quick recap and the competitive extract you asked for. I captured the rough qualification we discussed and a recommended next-step pricing approach. See bullets and attachments.

Call highlights (quick):
- Monthly token expectation: initial pilot 5M tokens/mo, scaling to 35–45M tokens/mo within 6–9 months (customer estimate).
- Workload: mostly generation (multi-turn chat) with ~30% embeddings for search/rerank. Typical peak latency constraint: p95 <= 450ms for single-shot prompts.
- Procurement/budget range: $6k–$30k/month depending on usage and commitment; procurement indicated they need a $/1M anchor to compare.

Target unit-econ benchmarking (per your ask — $/1M tokens):
- Redwood proposal (pilot): $6.0 — $7.5 / 1M tokens (pilot pricing + limited commitment, includes prefix caching and 2x free trial credit)
- Redwood committed rate (12-mo renewal): $4.5 — $6.0 / 1M tokens (volume tiers, dedicated pools, quantized model options)
- OpenAI (public endpoint, approximate list): ~$14 / 1M tokens (general-purpose; varies by model & context length)
- Anthropic (public endpoint, approximate): ~$12—$14 / 1M tokens
- Self-hosted open-models (infra-only estimate): $3 — $8 / 1M tokens (model-dependent; hidden ops, monitoring, infra overhead not included)

Notes/context on numbers:
- Our $/1M splits assume typical prompt/response lengths from your session (avg 300 tokens per interaction). If you push longer contexts or heavy streaming, effective $/1M will change.
- The \"self-hosted\" low end requires aggressive quantization + spot GPU mix and assumes mature SRE and model ops — many teams underestimate ops and support costs.
- Redwood competitive position aims to sit meaningfully below the hosted incumbents on unit cost while delivering managed SLAs and less ops burden than self-hosting.

Suggested immediate next steps:
1) I attached a compact comparator (redwood_token_model_comp.xlsx) with the per-1M math and assumptions. Please confirm if we should swap the avg token size assumption.
2) I'll draft a short pilot SOW (attached draft) for a 90-day pilot at the $6.5 / 1M pilot anchor with a 6-week ramp clause and clear KPIs.
3) If procurement wants, we can run a 2-week pilot at no-cost up to 5M tokens to validate latency/quality.

Would you like me to push a formal proposal and SOW this week? If so, I can get Marissa involved for commercial terms and Jordan to scope the Dedicated pool sizing.

Thanks — Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Solutions
Redwood Inference
---
Attachments referenced: redwood_token_model_comp.xlsx, MediaStream_SOW-draft.pdf


From: Alex Johnson <alex.j@mediastream.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@mediastream.io
Date: 2026-06-18T14:20:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Post-call recap: MediaStream pricing / target $/1M tokens

Ben — appreciate the quick turnaround and the transparent comparator. A few clarifying points from my side:
- Volume band looks right. We expect a conservative ramp: 5M in month 1, 20M by month 4, then 40M+ by month 9 if results hold.
- Budget note: leadership has signaled a $18k/month ceiling for Q3 pilots until they see latency/ROAS from the pilot.
- Procurement asked whether the $6.5 / 1M pilot rate is net of credits or if that includes the \"2x free credit\" you mentioned. Can you confirm what the invoiced rate would show?

Also, could you add a short line in the SOW about data residency (US-only) and an audit trail for inference logs for 90 days? Procurement requires explicit language on both for our initial legal review.

If you can get the proposal + SOW updated today, we can route to procurement and aim for a call with them Thursday morning.

— Alex
Alex Johnson
Head of ML Products | MediaStream


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Alex Johnson <alex.j@mediastream.io>
Date: 2026-06-18T14:35:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: Post-call recap: MediaStream pricing / target $/1M tokens — quick internal ask

Team — forwarding Alex's reply. Procurement wants the $6.5 pilot clarified (net-of-credits vs invoiced). Suggested approach:
- Show pilot commercial: list price $8.5 / 1M with an applied pilot credit that nets to $6.5 effective during the trial. That keeps the committed-rate ceiling intact for negotiation and preserves upside on conversion.
- For contract language: add a 90-day log retention clause (US-only region) and a simple extract for SOC2 scope & audit contacts. Legal will need the exact KMS/HSM wording but we can start with a stub.

Would also propose a conservative committed rate table for the SOW:
- 0–10M: $8.5 / 1M (list)
- 10–50M: $5.5 / 1M (committed tier)
- 50M+: $4.5 / 1M (enterprise commitment)

Marissa — can you draft the commercial terms to reflect the pilot credit structure + an express US-only data residency clause? Jordan — quick check on Dedicated pool sizing: assume baseline q8 quantized variant + 2x burst nodes for peak — does that match the 40M/mo target? If not, flag changes.

I'll incorporate those items into the SOW and send back to Alex and procurement.

Thanks — Ben


From: Alex Johnson <alex.j@mediastream.io>

Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, procurement@mediastream.io
Date: 2026-06-18T15:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Post-call recap: MediaStream pricing / target $/1M tokens

Thanks — that structure sounds fine. Procurement confirmed they want the SOW + a short one-page pricing annex that shows both the list and effective pilot net rate (so our finance team can see the discount mechanics). Could you get that to us by EOD today? If yes, procurement will schedule a short call Thursday 9:00am PT to walk contract items.

Also: one sticky point — leadership asked for a quick comparison vs \"self-hosted open model\" run by our infra team. They want to see the hidden ops risks and a TCO note. If you could add a single-paragraph TCO note to the Drive comparator that highlights ops/monitoring/latency risk assumptions, that would be helpful.

I'll expect the SOW + pricing annex and updated comparator (drive link) by EOD.

— Alex

title: Privacy triage: controller/processor confirmation + SCC module recommendation
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Sophie Martin <sophie.martin@ledgerwell.com>, Ledgerwell Legal <legal@ledgerwell.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 15 Jun 2026 14:22:00 -0700
Subject: Privacy triage / post-call recap — DPA, SCC module, controller/processor

Hi Sophie — thanks again for carving out time today. Quick recap and proposed next steps from our side so legal has everything to hand: 

Call summary (high level)
- Ledgerwell is evaluating Redwood for transaction enrichment and ledger reconciliation workflows (prod-facing).
- Primary privacy concerns: GDPR compliance across EU customers, decision on SCC module, and clarification on controller vs processor responsibilities.
- Secondary: SOC 2 evidence and HIPAA applicability for a subset of customers (you said a handful may be covered).

Proposed immediate asks / next steps
1) Confirm whether Ledgerwell prefers SCC Module 1 (controller-to-controller wording) or SCC Module 2 (controller-processor with supplementary terms). From the call it sounded like Module 2 may be preferred, but wanted written confirmation from legal.
2) We attached a draft DPA for your review (redwood_dpa_v1.docx) — please flag any redlines or required clauses.
3) We can start the vendor security questionnaire now; included a short version of our responses in vendor_security_questionnaire.pdf to speed review. If you prefer, we can also fill an Excel you send.
4) Controller/processor alignment: our default position (for hosted Redwood) is that Ledgerwell is the Controller and Redwood is the Processor. For private/on-prem deployments we can discuss dual-role nuances — see attached controller_processor_mapping.pdf for examples.

Timing: can we aim to have legal feedback on SCC module preference + any DPA redlines by EOW (Friday)? If that works I’ll draft a mutual action plan and loop Rishi (legal) + Laura (CS) to run the signatures.

Attachments referenced: redwood_dpa_v1.docx (DPA draft), vendor_security_questionnaire.pdf (pre-filled answers), controller_processor_mapping.pdf

Thanks — Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0192


From: Sophie Martin <sophie.martin@ledgerwell.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Ledgerwell Legal <legal@ledgerwell.com>
Date: Tue, 16 Jun 2026 09:15:00 +0100
Subject: Re: Privacy triage / post-call recap — DPA, SCC module, controller/processor

Ben — thanks for the clear summary. A couple quick notes from our side: 

- Ledgerwell legal prefers SCC Module 1 for initial review; they said Module 2 might add more friction for us because of our multi-tenant design. They asked us to get your recommendation in writing.
- We’ll run the DPA through our procurement queue; aiming to have first-pass comments by Thursday afternoon (16 Jun is fine but may slip to Friday depending on other reviews).
- Attached an internal privacy matrix (see link in-line) that shows which of our customers are in-scope for GDPR vs HIPAA. If there are particular clauses Redwood needs for HIPAA/BAA, please highlight them.

If you can confirm SOC 2 status and whether you have a redacted report available, that would speed the procurement review.

Thanks — Sophie
Sophie Martin
Head of Platform Ops, Ledgerwell
sophie.martin@ledgerwell.com

--- Forwarded attachment: (internal_privacy_matrix.xlsx)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Sophie Martin <sophie.martin@ledgerwell.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 17 Jun 2026 08:42:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Privacy triage / post-call recap — DPA, SCC module, controller/processor

On Tue, 16 Jun 2026 at 09:15 +0100, Sophie Martin wrote:
> Ben — thanks for the clear summary. A couple quick notes from our side: 
> - Ledgerwell legal prefers SCC Module 1 for initial review; they said Module 2 might add more friction for us because of our multi-tenant design.

Thanks Sophie — appreciate the quick reply. A few clarifications from our side: 

SOC 2 / HIPAA
- SOC 2: Redwood completed SOC 2 Type II in Q1 2025. We can share a redacted report and an executive summary with your procurement/legal team. Link: https://redwood-inference.com/soc2/redacted (we'll provide the document via secure link once NDA/POA is set).
- HIPAA / BAA: Redwood supports BAA and can execute a BAA for Ledgerwell customers that require it. For your HIPAA in-scope tenants we recommend private deployment or explicit contractual safeguards in the DPA/BAA.

SCC module recommendation
- Based on the call, Module 2 (controller-processor) maps better to our hosted model because Ledgerwell will be Controller for customer data and Redwood acts as Processor. That said, if Ledgerwell legal prefers Module 1 for contractual symmetry, we can accommodate — Rishi to confirm legal implications (see his note).

Controller/processor mapping & examples
- See attached controller_processor_mapping.pdf for three common patterns (hosted, dedicated, private). TL;DR: hosted = Ledgerwell controller / Redwood processor; private (customer-managed infra) = Ledgerwell controller / Redwood subprocessor (we discuss responsibilities in the DPA).

Next steps (proposed)
- Rishi: can Redwood legal provide quick guidance on Module 1 vs 2 implications and whether we should surface both options in the DPA?
- Sophie/legal: please share any redlines against redwood_dpa_v1.docx by EOD Friday so we can produce a mutual action plan.

Appreciate it — I’ll queue the SOC 2 exec summary for your legal mailbox once Rishi confirms distribution method.

Ben


From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
To: Sophie Martin <sophie.martin@ledgerwell.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 18 Jun 2026 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Privacy triage / post-call recap — DPA, SCC module, controller/processor

Hi Sophie / Ben — looping in final legal guidance. Short version: 

1) SCC modules: For hosted Redwood, Module 2 (controller-processor) is the accurate legal construct and eases operational controls. However, we will include Module 1 language as an alternate path if Ledgerwell needs parity language for downstream customers — that may require a small set of additional warranties.

2) DPA redlines: We prefer to keep the DPA focused on processor obligations + security measures. If Ledgerwell wants broader cross-guarantees (e.g., controller-level warranties) we can add them as optional clauses, but that will likely require procurement negotiation on indemnity language.

3) SOC 2 distribution: Ben — please share the redacted SOC 2 Type II report via the secure Drive link we listed (restrict to the Ledgerwell legal group). I will prepare a short cover note highlighting the control scope and relevant control owners.

4) Mutual action plan (proposed dates)
- Fri 2026-06-18: Ledgerwell provides DPA redlines and SCC module preference (target).
- Mon 2026-06-22: Redwood provides updated DPA with Module 2 as primary + Module 1 alternate language.
- Wed 2026-06-24: Procurement/legal sync meeting to resolve open indemnity/BAA items.

Please confirm these dates or propose alternatives. Happy to jump on a quick 30-minute call if that helps move things faster.

Regards,
Rishi Malhotra
Senior Counsel, Redwood Inference
rishi.malhotra@redwood.com
(attachment: controller_processor_mapping.pdf)

title: POC: Finlytics hands-on lab & staging prep — reschedule + load-test slots
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Samir Gupta <samir.gupta@finlytics.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 10 Jun 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: POC kick-off & environment check — proposed hands-on lab week of 6/15

Hi Samir —

Thanks again for agreeing to run the hands-on POC with Finlytics. We want to make the session practical (prompt review, routing rules, and light load tests) so we can validate latency/costs for your typical queries. To get the most out of the session we need a couple of environment items ahead of time:

- Staging endpoint (URL) + any allowlist IPs or VPC peering info so we can test routing.
- Sandbox API key(s) scoped to the test model.
- A short prompt set (5–10 prompts) you want us to run during the session.
- Preferred time windows for hands-on lab (we're proposing 60–90 minute blocks).

I'm available Tue/Wed/Thu afternoons PST next week (6/15–6/17). If those don't work, please suggest two alternate slots and we'll accommodate.

Attaching a quick pre-checklist that shows the minimal config we need.

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai | +1 415-555-0123
Redwood Inference

Attachment: finlytics-poc-precheck.pdf (staging checklist, API key instructions)

From: Samir Gupta <samir.gupta@finlytics.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Cc: Mia Chen <mia.chen@finlytics.com>, ops@finlytics.com
Date: Wed, 10 Jun 2026 10:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: POC kick-off & environment check — proposed hands-on lab week of 6/15

Ava —

Thanks for the checklist, and this sounds great. We can provide a staging URL and a scoped API key. Ops will need the allowlist format — copying ops and Mia who owns infra.

Proposed slots:
- Tue 6/15 1:00–2:30pm PT
- Wed 6/16 10:30–11:30am PT
- Thu 6/17 2:00–3:30pm PT (best for our engineering team)

We will also send a short example prompt set by EOD Thursday so you have it. Two quick questions:
1) For the load test portion, what concurrency levels do you normally run for these POCs?
2) Can you confirm we need to provide a VPC peering tunnel or will an allowlist of 3 IPs suffice for the staging tests?

—Samir
Samir Gupta
Head of Product, Finlytics
samir.gupta@finlytics.com | +1 212-555-0199

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Samir Gupta <samir.gupta@finlytics.com>
Cc: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 11 Jun 2026 08:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: POC kick-off & environment check — proposed hands-on lab week of 6/15

Samir —

Thanks — Thu 6/17 2:00–3:30pm PT works well for our engineers too. We'll plan for a 90-minute session focused on: prompt walkthrough (20–25 mins), routing & model variant discussion (20 mins), and a short load test run (30–35 mins) with time for Q&A.

To answer your questions:
- Typical POC concurrency: we run a step-up pattern: 1, 5, 10 concurrent clients for 60s each to observe latency and token throughput. If you expect very different traffic, tell us and we can adapt.
- Allowlist: three static IPs will be fine for the staging tests; if you need persistent integration later we'll talk VPC peering.

Attached is an allowlist template (staging-allowlist-template.txt). For API keys please create a sandbox key labeled finlytics-poc-2026 and share it as a secret via your vault or reply to ops with the key.

We'll use the following agenda and will circulate a shared Drive doc with the prompt set once you send it.

Agenda (90m):
- 00:00–00:10: welcome & objectives
- 00:10–00:35: prompt review & prompt-engineering tips
- 00:35–00:55: routing, model variants, and cost/latency tradeoffs
- 00:55–01:25: light load tests (step-up concurrency) + monitoring
- 01:25–01:30: next steps / mutual action items

See you on 6/17.

Ava

Attachment: staging-allowlist-template.txt

From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Samir Gupta <samir.gupta@finlytics.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 15 Jun 2026 11:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: POC kick-off & environment check — proposed hands-on lab week of 6/15

Quick technical note —

We'll bring a lightweight load harness that runs streaming generation, so the test will exercise both prefix cache behavior and streaming latency. For the load test we will:
- Start with single client (baseline latency)
- Ramp to 5 clients (observe batching efficiency)
- Ramp to 10 clients if the staging instance can handle it (we'll watch GPU utilization)

If Finlytics wants token limits during the test, please provide max_tokens per prompt (default we'll use 256). Also: if your staging endpoint terminates TLS with an appliance, please share the CA bundle ahead of time — otherwise the harness will fail TLS validation.

-Dev
Dev Patel
Senior SRE, Redwood Inference

title: Mutual Success Ledger — acceptance triggers & smoke validation runbook
preview:
From: Alice Dunn <alice@evergreenhealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Rajiv Singh <rajiv@evergreenhealth.com>
Date: Mon, 14 Jun 2026 10:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Trial acceptance criteria — next steps

Hi Ben,

Thanks again for the demo yesterday — the team is excited and wants to lock down what 'success' looks like for our 6-week evaluation before legal and procurement sign the MAP. Two asks from my side so we can move forward:

1) A concise list of measurable acceptance tests we can hand to our engineers (pass/fail thresholds).
2) A short runbook for the smoke validation we can execute in week 1 to validate connectivity, latency, and basic quality.

Procurement will also need the list included in the MAP so we can align internal sign-off. Can you and your team draft? We’re targeting a go/no-go checkpoint on 2026-07-27.

Thanks,
Alice Dunn
Head of Digital Innovation
Evergreen Health

----
Attachments: none


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Alice Dunn <alice@evergreenhealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 14 Jun 2026 12:18:00 -0700
Subject: Mutual Success Ledger + proposed smoke validation runbook (draft)

Alice — thanks, this is great. Per your request I've sketched a compact "Mutual Success Ledger" (attached as evergreen_map.xlsx) and a short smoke validation runbook (smoke_test_runbook.xlsx). Key pieces below so procurement can see the measurable items up front.

TL;DR (3 success pillars)
- Quality: model output concordance with clinical guidelines on our sample set (concordance >= 92% on the 200-case seed set).
- Reliability & latency: p95 token latency <= 180 ms for average query distribution; end-to-end request success >= 99.5% over 24h stability window.
- Integration & security: no PII exfiltration in randomized N=100 queries; VPN + VPC routing verified and audit logs visible within Redwood Console.

Exit gates (must be met for 'pass')
- Acceptance test suite: >= 92% concordance AND no critical failures in smoke runbook (see tests S1–S6).
- Throughput validation: sustained 50 rps for 10 minutes without errors, or agreed fallback plan enacted.
- Security sign-off: SOC2 artifacts reviewed and SOC2 snippets (attached) accepted by your security team.

Acceptance tests (concrete, replicable)
- S1 (Quality concordance): Run 200 seed prompts (shared Drive link). Pass if concordance >= 92% and false-positive medical advice < 2%.
- S2 (Latency smoke): Submit 100 representative queries at production concurrency; p95 latency < 180 ms.
- S3 (Stability window): 24-hour production-like traffic replay at 10–20 rps; success rate >= 99.5%.
- S4 (Security smoke): Run 100 randomized queries flagged for PII templates; zero confirmed exfiltration vectors.
- S5 (End-to-end cost check): Estimate tokens per session and projected monthly unit cost under target traffic; budget delta <= 20% of forecast.
- S6 (Failover check): Simulate model variant fallback and confirm automatic routing within 90s with no lost requests.

Runbook (week 1 smoke checklist — high level)
- Step 0: Provision keys and VPC peering (owners: Redwood infra, Evergreen netops).
- Step 1: Connectivity test — simple ping + TLS handshake logs.
- Step 2: Latency batch (S2) — run 100 queries and collect p50/p95/p99.
- Step 3: Quality seed (S1) — run 200-case seed set, capture disagreements + examples for review.
- Step 4: Security probes (S4) — run PII templates, inspect audit logs and KMS access events.
- Step 5: Throughput ramp (S3 & S6) — ramp to agreed rps and trigger fallback policy.

Owners & timeline
- Week 0 (now–Jun 21): Legal & security exchange MAP + SOC2 artifacts. Owner: Marissa (Redwood) & Rajiv (Evergreen).
- Week 1 (Jun 22–28): Smoke validation (S1–S4). Owners: Evergreen eng + Redwood SRE.
- Week 2–5 (Jun 29–Jul 26): Extended stability, cost measurement, integration tweaks. Weekly sync.
- Go/no-go checkpoint: 2026-07-27 — decision by Evergreen stakeholders.

Attachments: evergreen_map.xlsx (Mutual Success Ledger with traceable tests), smoke_test_runbook.xlsx (detailed commands), soc2_snippets.pdf. HubSpot deal RGW-9237 updated: https://app.hubspot.com/deals/12345678

If this looks right I’ll fold any edits into the MAP and send a one-page version for procurement.

—Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0182
Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Alice Dunn <alice@evergreenhealth.com>
Cc: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 15 Jun 2026 09:37:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Mutual Success Ledger + proposed smoke validation runbook (draft)

Ben — this draft is solid. A couple procurement/security clarifications to include in the MAP export for Evergreen procurement:

- Add a clause: "Acceptance testing artifacts (logs, sample outputs) retained for 30 days post-eval unless otherwise requested by customer." This makes legal comfortable about evidence.
- On S4 (Security): include exact audit log window (UTC) that Evergreen will review and who signs off. Recommend: logs available within 10 minutes of event; designated reviewers: Rajiv Singh + Evergreen security email list.
- On cost: include an explicit point about autoscaling behavior during the evaluation (max burst policy) and the projected capped spend for the evaluation period.

I attached a one-page MAP template (evergreen_map.xlsx sheet 2) and SOC2 snippets for quick review. Once Alice confirms, I’ll create the procurement-ready MAP and drop it in the shared Drive.

Marissa Cole
Director, GTM Operations — Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.com

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Alice Dunn <alice@evergreenhealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 14 Jun 2026 12:18:00 -0700
Subject: Mutual Success Ledger + proposed smoke validation runbook (draft)

Alice — thanks, this is great. Per your request I've sketched a compact "Mutual Success Ledger" (attached as evergreen_map.xlsx) and a short smoke validation runbook (smoke_test_runbook.xlsx). Key pieces below so procurement can see the measurable items up front.

TL;DR (3 success pillars)
- Quality: model output concordance with clinical guidelines on our sample set (concordance >= 92% on the 200-case seed set).
- Reliability & latency: p95 token latency <= 180 ms for average query distribution; end-to-end request success >= 99.5% over 24h stability window.
- Integration & security: no PII exfiltration in randomized N=100 queries; VPN + VPC routing verified and audit logs visible within Redwood Console.

Exit gates (must be met for 'pass')
- Acceptance test suite: >= 92% concordance AND no critical failures in smoke runbook (see tests S1–S6).
- Throughput validation: sustained 50 rps for 10 minutes without errors, or agreed fallback plan enacted.
- Security sign-off: SOC2 artifacts reviewed and SOC2 snippets (attached) accepted by your security team.

Acceptance tests (concrete, replicable)
- S1 (Quality concordance): Run 200 seed prompts (shared Drive link). Pass if concordance >= 92% and false-positive medical advice < 2%.
- S2 (Latency smoke): Submit 100 representative queries at production concurrency; p95 latency < 180 ms.
- S3 (Stability window): 24-hour production-like traffic replay at 10–20 rps; success rate >= 99.5%.
- S4 (Security smoke): Run 100 randomized queries flagged for PII templates; zero confirmed exfiltration vectors.
- S5 (End-to-end cost check): Estimate tokens per session and projected monthly unit cost under target traffic; budget delta <= 20% of forecast.
- S6 (Failover check): Simulate model variant fallback and confirm automatic routing within 90s with no lost requests.

Runbook (week 1 smoke checklist — high level)
- Step 0: Provision keys and VPC peering (owners: Redwood infra, Evergreen netops).
- Step 1: Connectivity test — simple ping + TLS handshake logs.
- Step 2: Latency batch (S2) — run 100 queries and collect p50/p95/p99.
- Step 3: Quality seed (S1) — run 200-case seed set, capture disagreements + examples for review.
- Step 4: Security probes (S4) — run PII templates, inspect audit logs and KMS access events.
- Step 5: Throughput ramp (S3 & S6) — ramp to agreed rps and trigger fallback policy.

Owners & timeline
- Week 0 (now–Jun 21): Legal & security exchange MAP + SOC2 artifacts. Owner: Marissa (Redwood) & Rajiv (Evergreen).
- Week 1 (Jun 22–28): Smoke validation (S1–S4). Owners: Evergreen eng + Redwood SRE.
- Week 2–5 (Jun 29–Jul 26): Extended stability, cost measurement, integration tweaks. Weekly sync.
- Go/no-go checkpoint: 2026-07-27 — decision by Evergreen stakeholders.

Attachments: evergreen_map.xlsx (Mutual Success Ledger with traceable tests), smoke_test_runbook.xlsx (detailed commands), soc2_snippets.pdf. HubSpot deal RGW-9237 updated: https://app.hubspot.com/deals/12345678

If this looks right I’ll fold any edits into the MAP and send a one-page version for procurement.

—Ben

Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0182

Date: Fri, 12 Jun 2026 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: Edgebase renewal: restructure proposal — commit mixing + interim credits

Hi Elaine,

Thanks again for taking the call yesterday. Per our conversation I put together a concise proposal that aims to keep your unit price stable through the renewal window while giving you flexibility to grow capacity without overcommitting upfront. High level:

- 12-month renewal term, starting 2026-07-01
- Mixed commit: 65% Base commit (monthly reserved units) + 35% on-demand buffer with capped overage rate
- Interim bridging credits (one-time) to offset the higher per-unit delta in months 1–3 as you transition usage patterns
- Optional 60-day price-hold on the base commit if Edgebase hits the agreed onboarding milestones (SLA onboarding, SSO, and data ingestion completion)

I attached a short proposal and the SOW draft. Key ask from your side: confirmation of expected q3 token/embedding volumes (even a range works) so we can size the base commit and the bridging-credit math.

- Edgebase_SOW_draft.docx (DOCX, 58KB)
- May_usage_report_edgebase.csv (CSV, 28KB)

If it helps, I can prepare two alternatives by EOD Monday: (A) more conservative base commit with larger bridge credit; (B) more aggressive commit with smaller credit but lower unit price. Which would you prefer us to prioritize?

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Elaine Park <elaine.park@edgebase.com>, Miguel Torres <m.torres@edgebase.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 16 Jun 2026 08:35:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Edgebase renewal: restructure proposal — commit mixing + interim credits

Elaine / Miguel —

Thanks for the questions. Quick answers inline, and below I pasted the updated proposal logic. Monica and Laura are on CC to confirm operational and invoicing items.

1) Price-hold conditions: the 60-day hold is milestone-driven only (onboarding, SSO, ingestion). We can include a soft usage threshold (>= 50% of committed units across the first 30 days) as an alternate trigger if you prefer — happy to make that optional.

2) Bridging credit application: the bridging credit is a one-time credit applied to invoices across months 1–3 as a dollar offset. It is calculated as the difference between the committed-price exposure and the blended actual usage, capped at the credit amount in the proposal. It is not a recurring true-up that modifies the committed quantity.

3) Overage cap: confirmed in writing — 1.75x list for on-demand overage, with a hard cap after 150% of baseline monthly usage (i.e., once usage exceeds 150% of baseline, we trigger rate review and short-term capacity increase).

Updated proposal summary (high level):
- Term: 12 months (2026-07-01 — 2027-06-30)
- Commit mix: 65% base reserved units (monthly), 35% ondemand buffer
- Unit economics: base unit price $0.0012/token equivalent; on-demand at 1.75x list up to 150% baseline
- Bridging credit: $45,000 applied across months 1–3 as invoice credits
- Optional milestone-based 60-day price hold (or alternative usage trigger)
- Invoicing: monthly in arrears; SOW attaches billing schedule for base commit and shows credit application rows

Attachments updated: Edgebase_Renewal_Proposal_v2.pdf (updated table on page 3) and SOW with milestone table. Laura — can you confirm the invoice line items and when the credit will appear (single line vs split months)? Monica — please confirm the onboarding milestones and whether the 60-day hold language is operationally feasible.

Next steps proposed:
- Miguel: indicate preferred trigger for the price-hold (milestone-only vs milestone+usage) and confirm procurement timeline.
- Edgebase: confirm a range for expected Jul–Sep usage (low/expected/high) — we only need ranges to finalize the commit split math.
- Redwood: we'll produce a clean redline of the MSA with the pricing exhibit for review by EOD Wed.

Thanks,
Ben

title: Post-call qualification: recommended model slate + perf evidence pack
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Sarah Jones <sarah@horizenhealth.com>, Tom Ellis <t.ellis@horizenhealth.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 16 Jun 2026 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: Post-call qualification: recommended model slate + perf evidence pack

Thanks again for the demo time today — good conversation and helpful context on the low-latency triage flows and the regulatory constraints for PHI handling. Quick recap and the concrete artifacts I promised:

Summary (2 lines):
- Recommended starting slate: a quantized 13B variant for most flows, a 7B cost-first model for high-concurrency endpoints, plus an optional 34B for best-quality summaries.
- Attached and linked: Redwood-verified performance profiles (TPS + latency percentiles), prompt set we used for benching, and raw traces for your review.

Recommended slate (prioritized):
- rw-13b-q4f (quantized 13B) — balance of throughput and instruction-following. Redwood-verified: throughput ~620 tokens/sec (avg), median p50 per-request latency 180ms (512-token gen), p90 520ms, p99 1.1s on c6-gpu-80 (benchmark config).
- mistral-7b-v1 (fp16) — ~1.2x lower cost, higher concurrency: throughput ~980 tokens/sec, p50 140ms, p90 420ms, p99 900ms (good for chat-heavy, short-response routes).
- redwood-34b-v1 (fp16) — highest quality lane for clinical summarization; throughput ~220 tokens/sec, p50 420ms, p90 1.2s, p99 2.6s. Use only for async summarization or batched jobs.

Key notes on the numbers above:
- Metrics are for 512-token generation scenarios (the same prompt set attached). We include both tokens/sec (system throughput) and per-request latencies to help you map to SLOs.
- Benchmarks include continuous batching and KV-cache enabled; your real-world numbers will vary with average response length and prefix reuse.

Quality/regression concerns we highlighted on call:

Recommended slate (prioritized):

- mistral-7b-v1 (fp16) — ~1.2x lower cost, higher concurrency: throughput ~980 tokens/sec, p50 140ms, p90 420ms, p99 900ms (good for chat-heavy, short-response routes).
- redwood-34b-v1 (fp16) — highest quality lane for clinical summarization; throughput ~220 tokens/sec, p50 420ms, p90 1.2s, p99 2.6s. Use only for async summarization or batched jobs.

- Metrics are for 512-token generation scenarios (the same prompt set attached). We include both tokens/sec (system throughput) and per-request latencies to help you map to SLOs.

title: Mapping service-provider lanes to data residency (CPRA addendum)
preview:
From: Jillian Hart <jillian.hart@greenmart.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Cc: 
Date: 2026-06-14T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: CPRA addendum: mapping 'service provider lanes' to data residency

Hi Ben/Monica,

Thanks for the demo last week. We're preparing a CPRA addendum for our legal team and have a couple of asks around how Redwood classes different processing activities — specifically what our legal team calls 'service provider lanes' (primary inference, telemetry, aggregated analytics). Attached is a draft addendum and a simplified dataflow diagram.

Questions:
- Can you confirm Redwood is comfortable with 'service provider' classification for inference-only flows that contain customer PII? (we need limited-use assurances)
- For telemetry and aggregated usage metrics, do you require a separate subprocessor clause or can that be covered under the same SP lane with additional constraints?
- Is there an option to restrict residency of California-origin data to US-only or CA-region endpoints? If not, what language would you accept instead?

We've attached CPRA_Addendum_v0.3.docx and a diagram. Could you respond with proposed redlines or schedule 30m to resolve? We have an internal procurement timeline to sign by end of month.

Thanks,
Jillian Hart
Head of Privacy Operations
GreenMart
Attachments: CPRA_Addendum_v0.3.docx (docx), dataflow-diagram.png (png)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Jillian Hart <jillian.hart@greenmart.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-06-14T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: CPRA addendum: mapping 'service provider lanes' to data residency

Jillian — thanks for sending this over. Looping in Aditya (privacy counsel) who can confirm legal stance and propose precise redlines.

Quick answers while Aditya reviews:
- Inference-only flows: Redwood's default position is 'service provider' / limited use; we don't monetize customer content.
- Telemetry: generally covered as operational telemetry under SP, but we can add stricter constraints (aggregation, de-identification) if needed.
- Residency: we offer regional routing policies for Dedicated/Private customers; hosted customers can request US-only routing but we need to document in the addendum.

Aditya — can you propose short redlines for sections 2.1/3.4 and a residency clause alternative? Monica — please attach the canonical telemetry definition we use.

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
Redwood Inference

From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Jillian Hart <jillian.hart@greenmart.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-06-15T09:20:00-07:00
Subject: Re: CPRA addendum: mapping 'service provider lanes' to data residency

Hi all — see proposed redlines below (inline) and rationale. I'm pasting key excerpts from CPRA_Addendum_v0.3 and adding suggested language.

On Mon, Jun 14, 2026 at 9:12 AM Jillian Hart <jillian.hart@greenmart.com> wrote:
> (original message text)...

Proposed redlines (high level):
- Section 2.1 (Role): Replace first sentence with: \"Provider shall act solely as a Service Provider (as defined in applicable US state privacy law) with respect to Personal Information provided by Customer for model inference, and shall use such Personal Information only to provide the services under this Agreement and as otherwise necessary to comply with law.\"
- Telemetry: Add new para 3.4.2: \"Telemetry and usage metrics shall be limited to aggregated, pseudonymized metrics and shall not be used to infer individual-level profiles or for advertising or resale. Provider may process telemetry for service quality and security, subject to Customer's right to opt-out for telemetry that contains Customer-identifiable Personal Information.\"
- Residency alternative: Add optional clause: \"Customer may request US-only routing for data originating from the United States. Provider will use commercially reasonable efforts to route and store such data in US-based regions; if Provider cannot comply, Provider will notify Customer and propose mitigations (encryption at rest, limited access controls).\"

Notes:
- The 'service provider' label is standard; customers sometimes want a stricter 'processor' enumeration — we can accept that language if GreenMart requires explicit 'no use for any other purpose' wording.
- For hosted accounts, routing guarantees are best-effort unless Customer purchases Dedicated or Private deployment.

I've attached a redlined copy named CPRA_Addendum_v0.3_Redline.docx (see attached). Happy to jump on a 20m call to finalize.

Regards,
Aditya Rao
Senior Privacy Counsel
Redwood Inference
Attachments: CPRA_Addendum_v0.3_Redline.docx

From: Jillian Hart <jillian.hart@greenmart.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-06-15T16:45:00-07:00
Subject: Re: CPRA addendum: mapping 'service provider lanes' to data residency

Thanks Aditya — this is helpful and close to what we need.

Two quick asks before we route to procurement:
1) Can you confirm the redline will include the telemetry opt-out mechanism and how that will be implemented (API flag / contract note)? We need to show procurement the operational control.
2) If Redwood accepts the US-only routing clause as an addendum option, can you add a sentence that you will notify GreenMart within 5 business days if routing cannot be achieved and provide mitigations?

We are available for a 20m call tomorrow (Jun 16) at 10:00 PST or 3:00 PST. Also please send a DocuSign link for the addendum once redlines are agreed — procurement prefers that for expedited signature.

Best,
Jillian Hart
Head of Privacy Operations
GreenMart

Thanks for taking the time last week to evaluate Redwood. Per our conversation I want to set up a technical deep-dive focused on integrating the Hosted API into QuantumCare's ingestion and streaming pipeline. Proposed goals:
- Walk through SDK options (Python/Node), streaming usage patterns, and continuity for long-lived sessions
- Demonstrate function/tool calling and structured output handling for your workflow

Thanks team — quick tech prep from my side. Suggested demo plan and prework items for QuantumCare engineers:

- 10–35m: SDK deep-dive (Python streaming, Node SDK patterns) + live streaming demo showing partial token streaming and reconnect semantics

title: Supplementary credits for OptiMed head-to-head evaluation
preview:
From: Sara Lopez <sara@optimed.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-06-10T09:14:00-07:00
Subject: Request: additional credits for head-to-head evaluation

Ben,

Thanks again for the demo yesterday — the latency profiles looked promising. We have an internal bake-off next week where we'll run a side-by-side against two incumbents. Procurement asked if Redwood can provide a short-term top-up of credits so the engineering team can run extended throughput tests and a few long-sequence conversations.

Current ask: 150k token-equivalent credits plus a 14-day trial extension. We will share the comp test harness and metrics (attached). If you can approve, procurement will sign a short MSA addendum.

Quick answers to help: timeline is 6/15–6/28, primary contact for technical setup is Marcus Li (marcus@optimed.com).

Appreciate any help — this is a competitive bake-off and the procurement scoring favors vendors who provide reproducible test capacity.

Best,
Sara Lopez
Head of ML Ops
OptiMed Inc
Attachments: OptiMed_eval_plan_v2.pdf, comp-eval-metrics.xlsx

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Sara Lopez <sara@optimed.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-06-10T10:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Request: additional credits for head-to-head evaluation

Sara — thanks for the details and the attachments. Looping in Marissa (sales lead) and Karthik (sales ops/finance) to confirm what we can offer for a short, competitive eval like this.

Couple of quick clarifying Qs for procurement:
- Is the evaluation restricted to non-production data?
- Will OptiMed require API keys tied to an MSA, or is a promo code and scoped project acceptable?

We typically handle these with a promo credit allocation plus a short trial extension; if OptiMed wants enterprise billing or specific contractual terms we can fast-track via a one-page addendum.

Ben
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
Phone: +1 415-555-0193

From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Cc: Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-06-11T08:21:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Request: additional credits for head-to-head evaluation

Ben — thanks for flagging. Context for approval: OptiMed is mid-stage (PMF) and this is a direct RFP where credit availability materially affects short-listing. I am comfortable approving an incentive but want it tied to measurable deliverables (test schedule + post-eval feedback). Proposed offer: 125k token credits + a 10-day extension, conditional on a short results debrief and the comp metrics file returned.

Karthik — please confirm if 125k fits within our promo guardrails and whether we should require a signed addendum. Evelyn, can you confirm the technical onramp (project scoping and key rotation details)?

Marissa
Director of Sales, Redwood Inference

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-06-11T10:02:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Request: additional credits for head-to-head evaluation

Marissa — 125k credits is OK from a promo budget perspective for a deal of this profile, provided we get the debrief and fill the short receipt form for promos. Recommend requiring the addendum (one-page) to capture the trial window and data use (non-production only). I'll draft the addendum and a promo receipt email template.

Evelyn — if we scope a single project with an API key and prefix/KV cache disabled for fairness, will that work? Also confirm whether we should provision a seeded KV for warm-prefix tests or advise them to use cold starts.

Karthik
Sales Operations, Redwood Inference

From: Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Cc: Sara Lopez <sara@optimed.com>
Date: 2026-06-12T11:47:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Request: additional credits for head-to-head evaluation

Ben / team / Sara — technical notes:

preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Alicia Torres <alicia@commerceq.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 08 Jun 2026 09:02:00 -0700
Subject: Hosted API capacity addendum & ops checklist

Hi Alicia —

Thanks again for the productive renewal discussion last week. Per our call, attached is the addendum that captures the change in monthly committed usage and the operational guardrails we discussed. High-level summary: 
- Monthly committed tokens: moving from 10M to 25M tokens/month
- Peak rateband: baseline 6 RPS (requests/sec) across your primary keys, with a burst allowance up to 18 RPS for 10-minute windows
- Soft-throttle behavior on sustained 2x above baseline (configurable)

I’ve also included a draft ops checklist (ops-runbook-v1.md) for your platform team — it documents warm-up guidance, KV-prefix caching recommendations, and an emergency fallback to a compact model variant we can route to automatically during capacity constraints.

Next steps I propose: 
1) Review addendum and runbook with your procurement and SRE teams 2) Sign the addendum (DocuSign link included) 3) I’ll coordinate a brief technical handoff call with Karthik to walk your SREs through warm-up and rate-limiting knobs

If that sequence works, can you confirm procurement contact and an internal date to complete sign-off?

Thanks —
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
Attachments: redwood-commit-addendum-2026.pdf, ops-runbook-v1.md

Cc: procurement@commerceq.com

Subject: Re: Hosted API capacity addendum & ops checklist

Ben, thanks — I’ve shared the addendum with our SRE lead and procurement. A couple quick clarifying items from our side before routing to legal/procurement: 

1) Billing cadence: will the delta (15M extra tokens) be billed pro-rata for the remainder of our term or start at the next billing cycle?

3) Rate limiting: how will the soft-throttle manifest (429 vs queued requests)? We prefer queued requests where possible to avoid client-side errors.

Also looping procurement email above (procurement@commerceq.com).

—Alicia

Head of Platform | CommerceQ

Date: Wed, 10 Jun 2026 08:46:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Hosted API capacity addendum & ops checklist

Alicia — great questions. Answers below and I’ve updated the addendum to reflect the SLA line and billing note; see attached PDF. Quick summary: 

1) Billing cadence: The added commit will be pro-rated for the current billing period and reflected on the next invoice as an adjustment line. We can alternatively backdate to the start of the next billing cycle if procurement prefers — let me know.

2) SLA: For the us-west region we’re committing to P99 <= 350ms for generation endpoints under the agreed committed capacity (25M tokens/month) with circuit-breaker policies documented in the ops runbook. I added that sentence to section 3 of the addendum.

3) Soft-throttle: the default behavior is to queue short-lived requests up to a 3s window before returning 429s. If you’d prefer request queuing disabled and an immediate 429, we can flip that per-key. I added a note in the runbook on how to toggle this via the console and API.

Estimated monthly spend (ARR run-rate): $12,400/mo (based on your historical usage pattern + new commit). Let procurement know if you want a line-itemed estimate vs a single net figure.

Next: can we schedule a 30-min SRE handoff on Thursday or Friday? Karthik will lead the session to walk through warm-up and KV-cache hints.

Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
Attachments: redwood-commit-addendum-2026.pdf (updated)


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
To: Alicia Torres <alicia@commerceq.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 12 Jun 2026 09:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Hosted API capacity addendum & ops checklist — SRE handoff prep

Hi Alicia — quick technical note ahead of the handoff: 

- Warm-up: we recommend a staggered ramp for active pods across your primary keys (start at 25% of expected QPS for 15 minutes, then 50% for 10 minutes, then full). This avoids cold KV-cache misses and reduces model kernel swaps.
- Rate limits: in your scenario (burst allowance to 18 RPS), we suggest applying token-based throttling at the per-api-key level to preserve per-request latency for smaller calls. Example: limit long-running generate calls to 12 RPS steady-state, allow short summarize calls to spike.
- Fallbacks: we will enable an automatic route to the compact-1 model variant when sustained queue depth exceeds threshold for 120s. The compact-1 variant is about ~20% cheaper and has ~1.4x lower latency.

I’ll bring these slides into the handoff. How does Friday 11:00am PT look for you?

-Karthik
Principal Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference

title: HavenRx production regressions after model v2.3 quantization
preview:
From: Laura Mendez <laura.mendez@havenrx.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Mike Johnson <mike.johnson@havenrx.com>, sre@havenrx.com
Date: Mon, 14 Jun 2026 08:12:04 -0700
Subject: Production: sharp latency + error spike after model update

Ava —

We're seeing a serious degradation in production since the model upgrade rolled on Friday night (redwood model v2.3 with the new quantization profile). Key impacts:
- p50 unchanged, but p99 latency jumped from ~420ms to ~1.8s over the weekend
- error rate (5xx) rose to ~4.8% on certain heavy-traffic endpoints
- throughput dropped ~22% under sustained load; timeouts reported by our downstream processing

This is live traffic for our med-prioritization flow — it's directly impacting response SLAs and our internal handling. We need an immediate plan: rollback, mitigation, or hotfix. Our SRE can open a call if helpful.

Please treat as high priority.

— Laura
Laura Mendez
Sr. Engineering Manager, Platform
HavenRx

Attachments: none in this message


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Laura Mendez <laura.mendez@havenrx.com>
Cc: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 14 Jun 2026 08:20:17 -0700
Subject: Re: Production: sharp latency + error spike after model update

Hi Laura — thank you for flagging and sorry for the disruption. We're pulling the on-call and diagnostics right now.

Immediate next steps:
1) I've looped in our infra lead (David) and VP Eng (Marcus) — we'll triage logs and determine whether a rollback is needed.
2) Could you share the precise hit window + a sample request id? That will speed root cause.
3) If you prefer, let's jump on a 30-min call — I'm available 08:30 or 09:00 PT.

We'll prioritize the safety path (rollback to last known good) if it looks like QoS/latency regression rather than an edge case in your inputs.

— Ava Chen
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0123

On Mon, Jun 14, 2026 at 08:12 AM Laura Mendez <laura.mendez@havenrx.com> wrote:
> Ava —
>
> We're seeing a serious degradation in production since the model upgrade rolled on Friday night (redwood model v2.3 with the new quantization profile). Key impacts:
> - p50 unchanged, but p99 latency jumped from ~420ms to ~1.8s over the weekend
> - error rate (5xx) rose to ~4.8% on certain heavy-traffic endpoints
> - throughput dropped ~22% under sustained load; timeouts reported by our downstream processing
>
> This is live traffic for our med-prioritization flow — it's directly impacting response SLAs and our internal handling. We need an immediate plan: rollback, mitigation, or hotfix. Our SRE can open a call if helpful.
>
> Please treat as high priority.
>
> — Laura


From: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 14 Jun 2026 08:45:33 -0700
Subject: Re: Production: sharp latency + error spike after model update (technical notes)

Folks — quick triage summary from the runtime team.

What we observed: the v2.3 model with the new quantization profile (q3->q4 mixed path) introduces a different decode kernel path that increases per-token CPU/GPU synchronization under the beam/ sampling config used by HavenRx. Under typical sequences the KV cache expands differently and memory paging between CUDA memory + host becomes more frequent at the tail latencies.

Measured from Canary -> Prod telemetry:
- baseline (v2.2, q3_profile): p99 ~420ms, error ~0.3%
- post-upgrade (v2.3, q4_mixed): p99 ~1.8s, 5xx errors to 4.8% concentrated on requests >512 tokens
- throughput per GPU decreased ~18-25% depending on batch mixing

Root cause hypothesis: the quantization selection pushed some attention kernels to a slower fallback that greatly impacts longer sequences. There are also memory fragmentation effects that cause transient OOMs->5xx.

Immediate remediation options (ranked by speed of mitigation):
A) Roll back to v2.2 (last known good) — fastest, <30m to flip and validate if we have hot spares and the model artifacts cached.
B) Adjust quantization profile to q4_light (tighter bias toward q3 path) — medium effort, ~2-4 hours to build/test and canary.
C) Apply request-side throttling / drop long-sequence sampling for the next 2 hours — quick but degrades quality for those inputs.

Attachments: p99_latency_report_20260614.csv (aggregated telemetry), error_trace_645.log (sample 5xx trace).

Recommendation: Start with rollback (A) to stabilize production, then implement B as the long-term fix with canary. I'll prepare a rollback playbook (rollback_playbook.md) and target a rollback window once we get sign-off.

— David
David Kim
Principal Software Engineer, Serving Runtime
Redwood Inference

On Mon, Jun 14, 2026 at 08:20 AM Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com> wrote:
> Hi Laura — thank you for flagging and sorry for the disruption. We're pulling the on-call and diagnostics right now.

To: Leadership Distribution List (internal)
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 14 Jun 2026 09:05:00 -0700
Subject: EXEC: HavenRx production regression — propose immediate rollback + customer credit discussion

Team — looping in leadership because this is high visibility with HavenRx (large strategic account on Dedicated capacity). Impact: multiple customer endpoints degraded, potential SLA breach.

Summary: runtime analysis indicates the new v2.3 quantization profile is the likely cause (see David's notes and attached telemetry). David recommends rollback to v2.2 as the fastest stabilization.

Ask: approval for immediate rollback and an incident call with HavenRx at 09:30 PT. Customer will likely request a credit; please advise on policy for a strategic account. Preliminary containment estimate: rollback + validation within 30–45 minutes if we have cache artifacts; full postmortem and mitigation plan within 48 hours.

Proposed customer-facing remedies:
- Immediate rollback to v2.2 on their production host
- 10% invoice credit for the impacted billing window (to be finalized by finance)
- Joint postmortem + technical walkthrough within 48 hours

I'm authorizing the runtime team to proceed with rollback when David says ready. Evelyn — please prepare CS comms; Jonas — please prepare a short product note on model rollout guardrails we can share.

— Marcus Lin
VP, Engineering — Serving
Redwood Inference

Cc: Mike Johnson <mike.johnson@havenrx.com>, sre@havenrx.com

From: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 14 Jun 2026 08:45:33 -0700
Subject: Re: Production: sharp latency + error spike after model update (technical notes)

Folks — quick triage summary from the runtime team.

What we observed: the v2.3 model with the new quantization profile (q3->q4 mixed path) introduces a different decode kernel path that increases per-token CPU/GPU synchronization under the beam/ sampling config used by HavenRx. Under typical sequences the KV cache expands differently and memory paging between CUDA memory + host becomes more frequent at the tail latencies.

Measured from Canary -> Prod telemetry:
- baseline (v2.2, q3_profile): p99 ~420ms, error ~0.3%
- post-upgrade (v2.3, q4_mixed): p99 ~1.8s, 5xx errors to 4.8% concentrated on requests >512 tokens
- throughput per GPU decreased ~18-25% depending on batch mixing

Root cause hypothesis: the quantization selection pushed some attention kernels to a slower fallback that greatly impacts longer sequences. There are also memory fragmentation effects that cause transient OOMs->5xx.

Immediate remediation options (ranked by speed of mitigation):
A) Roll back to v2.2 (last known good) — fastest, <30m to flip and validate if we have hot spares and the model artifacts cached.
B) Adjust quantization profile to q4_light (tighter bias toward q3 path) — medium effort, ~2-4 hours to build/test and canary.
C) Apply request-side throttling / drop long-sequence sampling for the next 2 hours — quick but degrades quality for those inputs.

Attachments: p99_latency_report_20260614.csv (aggregated telemetry), error_trace_645.log (sample 5xx trace).

Recommendation: Start with rollback (A) to stabilize production, then implement B as the long-term fix with canary. I'll prepare a rollback playbook (rollback_playbook.md) and target a rollback window once we get sign-off.

— David
David Kim
Principal Software Engineer, Serving Runtime
Redwood Inference

title: Connector sweat-equity pilot: scope & next steps
preview:
From: Samir Patel <samir@atlasdata.co>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Olivia Marsh <olivia@pinecone.io>
Date: 2026-06-10T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Pilot proposal — connector sweat-equity model

Hi Ben,

Great meeting yesterday — thanks for taking the time. AtlasData is interested in a small, focused pilot to validate Redwood's connector pattern (pgvector/S3 fallback + Pinecone fast-path) against a live customer workload. Our ask: we provide three medium-sized indexes and a couple of real query workloads; you provide engineering time to harden the connector and a short co-sell GTM commitment.

We'd be open to a \"sweat-equity\" arrangement — AtlasData contributes data and integration tests, Redwood contributes prioritized engineering effort and a commercial credit for co-sell. Success criteria we'd like to validate in the pilot:
- End-to-end query latency P95 under 200ms on Pinecone fast-path for top-50 queries
- Transparent spillover to S3/pgvector cold lane with <500ms additional latency
- No data residency violations (EU data samples excluded)
- A reproducible migration procedure (runbook) we can use for customers

I've attached a short pilot-outline.pdf with the workloads and a rough timeline. Olivia from Pinecone is looped in to confirm any vendor-side constraints.

Can you share who on your side would own the technical intake and the ops runbook? We'd like to kick off the technical discovery next week.

Thanks,
Samir
Samir Patel
Head of Integrations, AtlasData
---
Attachment: pilot-outline.pdf (1 page)

Thanks Samir — this looks promising. Looping the small cross-functional team so we can scope quickly. Quick thoughts from my side:

- Technical intake owner: Connor — can you scope the connector changes required to support AtlasData's indexing pattern (embedding sizes + realtime streaming)?
- Ops/runbook: Laura — this is a good candidate for a co-authored runbook (Redwood + AtlasData). We need explicit rollback and failover steps.
- Commercial: Vanessa — please propose a simple commercial structure (credits + short-term co-sell commitment).

My priorities for the pilot are: producing a deterministic migration path (no surprises for customers), validating the S3 cold-lane latency guarantees, and creating a test harness that AtlasData can reuse.

Samir — can you confirm the three indexes are non-EU and that we can run the workloads against a staging Pinecone project? Also flag any IP concerns you have about tests/data.

title: Post-demo recap: connection pooling & quota alignment (BoltAI)
preview:
From: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwood.ai>
To: Eric Tan <eric.tan@boltai.com>, Sofia Morales <sofia.morales@boltai.com>
Cc: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 10 Jun 2026 13:12:00 -0700
Subject: Post-demo recap: connection pooling & quota alignment (BoltAI)

Thanks again for the demo today — appreciated the clear perf targets. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we're aligned: 

Summary of what we heard from BoltAI:
- Streaming-first integration for inference responses (partial tokens delivered as they generate).
- Target first-token p95: ~150ms (for typical prompts, 512 token frontier).
- Sustained throughput anticipated during alpha: ~8k tokens/s, peak bursts up to ~20k tokens/s.
- Concurrency model: ~250 concurrent requests from the service side; per-endpoint concurrency per user expected 2–4.

Open questions we promised to follow up on:
1) How Redwood enforces rate limits vs connection-based concurrency (how quotas are counted: connections vs concurrent requests vs tokens).
2) Whether connection pooling / keepalive reduces quota consumption for streaming.
3) Temporary quota bump for alpha + how to dial Dedicated pool sizing for spikes.

Proposed next steps:
- Eric to share a simple load/profile CSV (format attached) with expected request size distribution and burst cadence.
- Redwood to respond with: a) recommended rate-limiting model, b) a pilot quota proposal (timeboxed bump), and c) Technical notes for streaming clients (keepalive, gRPC vs HTTP SSE).

Attachments: Bolt_load_profile.csv (you mentioned you'll confirm the numbers/content)

Thanks — looping in Aditya for the technical sizing.

—
Camila Reyes
Senior Sales Manager, Redwood Inference
camila.reyes@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0124

Attachment: SLA-rate-limits-proposal.xlsx (stub)

From: Eric Tan <eric.tan@boltai.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwood.ai>
Cc: Sofia Morales <sofia.morales@boltai.com>
Date: Thu, 11 Jun 2026 09:47:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Post-demo recap: connection pooling & quota alignment (BoltAI)

Thanks Camila — attaching our load profile and a short note on how our clients behave. See Bolt_load_profile.csv. Key clarifications: 

- Concurrency: 250 concurrent requests is our median expectation for the alpha environment; in peak scenarios (promo events) we could see short spikes to ~600 concurrent requests for a few minutes.
- Per-session concurrency: bots typically maintain 3 open streams per user for multi-turn interactions.
- Streaming: we need chunked streaming so UI can show partial answers. Latency to first chunk is the most important metric.
- Rate limits: we prefer not to have per-connection throttles that penalize long-lived streaming connections; ideally limits are token-based or request-based with exception handling for streaming sessions.

Request: Can Redwood provide a short pilot quota (30 days) that allows: 1) 350 nominal concurrent request quota, 2) token-rate limit of 25k tokens/s, plus graceful 429 behavior and documented retry windows?

Also, please confirm whether connection pooling/keepalives will reduce quota usage on your side if we reuse sockets (we use a shared HTTP/2 pool in front of our streaming clients).

Thanks — Eric
Eric Tan
Lead Infra Engineer, BoltAI
eric.tan@boltai.com
+1 (510) 555-4099

Cc: Eric Tan <eric.tan@boltai.com>
Date: Fri, 12 Jun 2026 09:07:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Post-demo recap: connection pooling & quota alignment (BoltAI)

Thanks for the context — quick technical proposal below. High level: Bolt's pattern (streaming + bursts) fits two options: Hosted with a measured pilot bump, or Redwood Dedicated pool for predictable SLOs.

Option A — Hosted (short pilot):
- Pilot bump (30 days): Increase concurrent request quota to 350, token-rate bump to 25k tokens/s. We can do this as a time-bound quota adjustment scoped to the Bolt API key(s).
- Rate-limit model: primary shaping on token-rate (tokens/s) + soft concurrent request cap. Streaming sessions are token-accounted as they emit tokens; long-lived sockets are not penalized unless they continuously emit tokens.
- Connection pooling: reusing HTTP/2 or gRPC connections reduces TCP/TLS overhead and helps our front-end node reuse runtime resources. However, quota accounting remains per-request/token emission — keepalives alone won't bypass token accounting but do reduce connection churn.
- 429 behavior: 429s will include a Retry-After header and an explicit JSON reason. For streaming, recommend exponential backoff with jitter and a linear fallback to short polling for degraded periods.

Option B — Dedicated pool (recommended for predictable p95/p99):
- Dedicated GPU pool sized for Bolt's sustained 8k tokens/s with headroom for 20k token/s bursts (autoscale policy). We can guarantee tighter p95 first-token latency by isolating capacity.
- Concurrency model: Capacity is defined in concurrent request slots per model variant + tokens/s shaping at the pool level. This avoids noisy-neighbor tail effects.

Client guidance (will send as a one-pager):
- Use HTTP/2 or gRPC with max concurrent streams per connection set to 16 (client-side) and a connection pool of N workers (depends on instance count).
- Keepalive: 60s ping interval is fine. Ensure idle connection timeouts align with your LB (recommended 120s).
- Streaming: request first-token timeout of 500ms client-side; if not met, surface partial fallback and allow server to complete when ready.

Next steps I propose:
1) Camila, confirm Bolt wants Hosted pilot or Dedicated evaluation.
2) If pilot: Ben/Finance to prepare a short-term pricing addendum (I can provide estimated committed units and expected uplift).
3) Technical: I'll draft the pilot quota doc (inc. exact 429 semantics, metrics to monitor, and rollback plan) and share by EOD Monday.

Happy to jump on a short call with Bolt infra if you want to walk through the streaming retry semantics.

—
Aditya Rao
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
aditya.rao@redwood.ai

Thanks Camila — attaching our load profile and a short note on how our clients behave. See Bolt_load_profile.csv. Key clarifications: 

- Concurrency: 250 concurrent requests is our median expectation for the alpha environment; in peak scenarios (promo events) we could see short spikes to ~600 concurrent requests for a few minutes.
- Per-session concurrency: bots typically maintain 3 open streams per user for multi-turn interactions.
- Streaming: we need chunked streaming so UI can show partial answers. Latency to first chunk is the most important metric.
- Rate limits: we prefer not to have per-connection throttles that penalize long-lived streaming connections; ideally limits are token-based or request-based with exception handling for streaming sessions.

Request: Can Redwood provide a short pilot quota (30 days) that allows: 1) 350 nominal concurrent request quota, 2) token-rate limit of 25k tokens/s, plus graceful 429 behavior and documented retry windows?

Also, please confirm whether connection pooling/keepalives will reduce quota usage on your side if we reuse sockets (we use a shared HTTP/2 pool in front of our streaming clients).

Thanks — Eric
